articles.title: Sharon Osbourne Wants Digital Ozzy to Be the New Elvis
articles.summary: Sharon and Jack Osbourne have announced plans for 'Digital Ozzy', an AI-powered interactive avatar of the late Ozzy Osbourne, built in partnership with Hyperreal and Proto Hologram. The avatar will appear through Proto Luma holoportation devices and be able to hold live conversations with fans in the US and UK starting late summer 2026. Sharon Osbourne compared the ambition to Elvis Presley's enduring legacy, saying 'I just want that for Ozzy.'
articles.content:
Ozzy Osbourne passed away in 2025, but his family has no intention of letting the Prince of Darkness go quietly. Sharon Osbourne and son Jack Osbourne announced this week at Licensing Expo 2026 in Las Vegas that they have partnered with digital entertainment company Hyperreal and hologram technology firm Proto Hologram to create 'Digital Ozzy', an AI-powered interactive avatar of the heavy metal legend.

The avatar will appear through Proto Hologram's Proto Luma devices, which are life-sized holoportation units designed to project digital avatars that can hold real conversations. Digital Ozzy is set to roll out across the United States and the United Kingdom in late summer 2026. Sharon described what fans can expect: 'You can ask Ozzy anything, and he will answer you in his own voice, and the answers will be what Ozzy would have said. People can talk to him and he will talk back.'

Jack Osbourne was equally candid about how convincing the technology already is. He said the digital creation carries 'the digital DNA of Ozzy Osbourne, voice, image and movement', and admitted the realism was unsettling. 'It's kind of scary how it's really very accurate,' Jack said, adding that Ozzy 'will exist digitally as himself for as long as we have computers.' The project draws exclusively from authenticated and approved source material, with the Osbourne family maintaining full creative control.

Sharon's reasoning for the whole project is hard to argue with. She pointed to Elvis Presley as the benchmark: 'Elvis died 50 years ago, and everybody knows Elvis. I just want that for Ozzy.' The comparison is deliberate. The Osbournes are not just preserving a memory. They are trying to build an ongoing, living cultural presence for one of rock's most beloved figures. Hyperreal had previously created an interactive avatar of Marvel icon Stan Lee that held live conversations with fans at LA Comic-Con, which Sharon reportedly saw firsthand before committing to the project.

The announcement has sparked serious debate online about the ethics of AI recreations of deceased artists. But the Osbourne family's hands-on approach, including their insistence that every element of Digital Ozzy was built from consented and approved material, sets this apart from more controversial posthumous AI projects. Whether you see it as a heartfelt tribute or a step too far, one thing is certain: the conversation around what happens to our icons after they die just got a lot more complicated.
keyPoints: Sharon and Jack Osbourne announced 'Digital Ozzy' at Licensing Expo 2026 in Las Vegas
The avatar is built with Hyperreal and Proto Hologram using Proto Luma holoportation devices
Digital Ozzy will be able to hold live conversations with fans in the US and UK from late summer 2026
All material used was authenticated and approved by the Osbourne family
Sharon Osbourne cited Elvis Presley's enduring legacy as the inspiration for the project

articles.title: Taylor Swift's No-Plus-One Rule Has Some Wedding Guests Refusing to Attend
articles.summary: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce's July 3 wedding in New York City has hit a snag, with some guests furious after learning their invitations do not include a plus-one. At least one unnamed guest says they are considering skipping the wedding entirely rather than showing up alone. The venue itself will only be revealed to guests on the morning of the ceremony.
articles.content:
Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce are getting married on July 3 in New York City, and it is already the most talked-about wedding of the year before a single vow has been exchanged. Taylor and Travis, both 36, announced their engagement on Instagram in August 2025 after dating for over two years. But not every invitee is feeling the love right now.

According to a May 22 report from the Daily Mail, Swift and Kelce have decided that some of their guests will not be allowed to bring a date. One unnamed guest made their feelings very clear. "My invite did not let me bring a plus one. I mean, what am I supposed to do? Go alone? That is so awkward. I don't think I am going to attend because I don't want to go by myself and I am not sure I will know too many people there. I mean, sorry, I am not friends with Gigi and Bella Hadid!"

The guest also pointed out the apparent inconsistency. "I know Selena Gomez is bringing her husband, Benny Blanco, so she has a plus one, but I am not allowed to have a plus one because I am a single woman. I get it, the venue can probably allow for only so many people, but it's not the best feeling." The venue itself adds another layer of mystery. Guests have yet to be told where the high-profile nuptials will take place. Instead, they will find out on the morning of July 3. "I get that too, she doesn't want everyone knowing where she is getting married until the last minute for security reasons," said one source.

An impressive list of celebrity attendees have reportedly already been invited, including Chiefs coach Andy Reid, Selena Gomez and Gigi Hadid, who are part of the bridal party, plus Zoë Kravitz and Graham Norton. Taylor's besties Selena Gomez, Bella and Gigi Hadid, Blake Lively, and Sabrina Carpenter are expected to attend, as well as Travis's Chiefs teammate Patrick Mahomes and his wife, Brittany. There is a lot of star power on the guest list, which makes the solo arrival anxiety a little more understandable.

Meanwhile, the wedding secrecy goes beyond just the venue. Taylor Swift reportedly regards Travis's father, Ed Kelce, as a "blabbermouth" who has already leaked several secrets about the nuptials. "He's sort of like a loose cannon," one source explained. "Nobody knows what he's going to say, so it's kind of a controversial situation." Swift is clearly running an extremely tight ship, and the no-plus-one rule is just one part of keeping the biggest wedding of 2026 as controlled as possible.
keyPoints: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce are marrying on July 3 in New York City
Some guests were not given a plus-one on their invitation and are considering not attending
The venue will only be revealed to guests on the morning of the wedding for security reasons
Celebrity attendees reportedly include Selena Gomez, the Hadid sisters, Zoë Kravitz, and Patrick Mahomes
Swift is reportedly keeping wedding details from Travis's father, Ed Kelce, over concerns he will leak them

articles.title: Marc Andreessen Can't Explain AI's Benefits When Joe Rogan Asks Him To
articles.summary: Marc Andreessen, the billionaire venture capitalist who has staked billions on AI, completely fumbled when Joe Rogan asked him to simply explain how AI will benefit humanity. Instead of a clear answer, Andreessen launched into a rambling analogy about Isaac Newton and alchemy before abandoning it entirely. The clip went viral, and critics say it reveals something important about the gap between AI hype and reality.
articles.content:
Marc Andreessen is one of the loudest voices in tech. He co-founded the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz, has invested billions into AI development, and has repeatedly declared that AI will save the world. So when Joe Rogan asked him on a recent podcast episode to just sell the idea of AI to a regular person, you would expect a polished answer. That is not what happened.

The conversation began with Andreessen asserting that tech executives have done a terrible job explaining what makes AI so important. "So, you're saying that the people running AI have done a terrible job of selling AI," Rogan said. "So sell it." Andreessen immediately stammered: "Yes — oh, sell it, I mean, look, so it, it is, alright — I mean, alright I'm gonna give you the deepest of all pitches, I'm gonna give you the, the — okay."

Andreessen then reached for an analogy, explaining that Isaac Newton spent 20 years searching for alchemy, trying to turn lead into gold. "In any event, you may know that he never — we have never figured out how to do that," Andreessen continued. "And gold is still rare and valuable, so, imagine a form of alchemy that turns sand into thought. Pause on that for a moment." It's barely comprehensible, to put it charitably.

Andreessen then immediately abandoned the alchemy analogy to explain that AI is actually good because things like lawsuits, medical problems, and work are just too hard for humans to think about on their own. "We're always all bumping up on these limitations on thought, like just how smart can we be, how many things can we know about, and so AI quite literally is that: it's thought at scale, for everybody, in perpetuity," he insisted.

Andreessen then closed with an appeal to the personal: "I guess I see this with my 11-year-old right now, like everybody who grows up now is going to have AI." Critics pointed out this is a self-reinforcing argument that does not actually explain why AI matters. The fact that one of AI's loudest and richest devotees can barely articulate why he thinks the technology is important should be a telling sign, especially as productive returns on the tech remain far out of reach.

Commenters posting under clips of the interview were unimpressed. "Before AI, if I didn't know something I would just google it," one TikTok user wrote. "AI gets to the answer faster but it's only correct half the time. How is that revolutionary?" It is a fair question. Andreessen Horowitz and its billionaire founders have put an unprecedented $115 million into influencing the 2026 midterm election cycle, much of it aimed at electing pro-AI legislators. When you are spending that much money on a technology's political future, being able to answer a basic question about its human benefits seems like the bare minimum.
keyPoints: Marc Andreessen stumbled badly when Joe Rogan simply asked him to explain AI's benefits on a recent podcast
Andreessen opened with a rambling Isaac Newton alchemy analogy before abandoning it mid-thought
His eventual answer was that AI is 'thought at scale, for everybody, in perpetuity'
Critics say the clip reveals how AI's biggest investors struggle to articulate concrete benefits
Andreessen Horowitz has committed $115 million to influence the 2026 midterms in favor of pro-AI candidates
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Marc Andreessen venture capitalist Andreessen Horowitz

articles.title: Eleven's Pink Dress From Stranger Things Season 1 Is Now at the Smithsonian
articles.summary: The Duffer Brothers and Shawn Levy have donated Eleven's pink dress from Stranger Things Season 1 to the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History, making it Netflix's first show with an item in the national collection. The signing ceremony took place at a Netflix FYC event at Hollywood Forever Cemetery in Los Angeles on May 21. Stranger Things wrapped its five-season run on New Year's Eve.
articles.content:
Stranger Things has added one more first to its already remarkable list. The Duffer Brothers, Matt and Ross, along with director and executive producer Shawn Levy, have officially donated Eleven's pink dress from Season 1 to the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History. The deed of gift was signed at a Netflix For Your Consideration event held at the Hollywood Forever Cemetery in Los Angeles on May 21, with cast members including David Harbour, Natalia Dyer, and Jamie Campbell Bower in attendance.

The pink dress is one of the most recognisable costumes in recent television history. Smithsonian entertainment curator Ryan Lintelman, who announced the donation at the event, explained exactly why this particular outfit matters so much. The costume was designed by Kimberly Adams and Malgosia Turzanska, and in the story it represents a turning point for Eleven, played by Millie Bobby Brown. Before putting on the dress, Eleven had only ever worn hospital gowns and hand-me-downs. When she first tried on the dress and a blonde wig, she said she looked pretty. It was the first real moment of self-expression for a character who had been controlled her entire life.

Strange Things wrapped its five-season run on New Year's Eve after becoming one of the defining shows of the streaming era. It holds a remarkable set of records for Netflix: it was the streamer's first original series to become a genuine global cultural phenomenon, the first series Netflix fully owned, and the first Netflix title to be turned into a full licensing empire spanning toys, games, live experiences, clothing, and more. Halloween costumes were the first area the show expanded into, and the pink dress was one of the most sought-after Halloween costumes of the entire 2016 season.

Now the dress joins the national collection at the Smithsonian, which puts Stranger Things in genuinely rare company as a pop culture artifact deemed worthy of preservation alongside American history. Shawn Levy, who has his own history with the Smithsonian having directed the 2009 film Night at the Museum: Battle of the Smithsonian, called the moment 'amazing.' Lintelman noted that the show's themes, including skepticism of authority, the power of friendship, and the idea that young people can see truths adults cannot, are ones that will resonate for generations.

It is a fitting send-off for a show that changed what streaming television could be. The dress does not just represent Eleven. It represents a moment when a small-town supernatural story about kids on bikes became something the whole world stopped to watch.
keyPoints: The Duffer Brothers and Shawn Levy donated Eleven's Season 1 pink dress to the Smithsonian
The signing happened at a Netflix FYC event at Hollywood Forever Cemetery on May 21
Stranger Things is now Netflix's first show with an item in the Smithsonian's national collection
The costume was designed by Kimberly Adams and Malgosia Turzanska
The pink dress was one of the most popular Halloween costumes of the 2016 season
Stranger Things finished its five-season run on New Year's Eve

articles.title: Olivia Rodrigo's 'the cure' Sounds Like Smashing Pumpkins, Not The Cure
articles.summary: Olivia Rodrigo dropped 'the cure' on Friday, the second single from her upcoming album you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love, out June 12. Despite the name, Rodrigo confirmed the song has nothing to do with Robert Smith's band The Cure, and critics are pointing out it owes far more to The Smashing Pumpkins. Rodrigo herself has called 'the cure' the thesis statement of the new album and one of her favourite songs she has ever made.
articles.content:
Olivia Rodrigo is back in the dark, and it sounds incredible. Rodrigo can't fix herself in time to save her relationship on 'the cure,' her new song that starts as an alt-pop ballad and builds to an anxiety attack. It is the kind of emotional escalation her fans fell in love with on her first two albums, Sour and Guts, but this time the production feels bigger and more cinematic than anything she has done before.

Although called 'the cure,' she confirmed it doesn't have anything to do with The Cure, and it probably owes more to The Smashing Pumpkins. The Cure, for the uninitiated, is the legendary British rock band fronted by Robert Smith, known for gothic and post-punk classics. The name overlap created obvious buzz before the release, but the song itself lands in very different sonic territory. It sounds like The Smashing Pumpkins, something from Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness, the iconic 1995 double album from Billy Corgan's band. Rodrigo and producer Dan Nigro use strings for racing heartbeat arpeggios on the track's back third, as well as during the comedown, where the instrumentation adds a bit of majesty that evokes 'Tonight, Tonight.'

She's about three and a half minutes into a five-minute track before it starts building up a real head of steam. That patience pays off in a big way. It actually goes somewhere, and sticks what we used to call a landing, which feels especially rewarding in a current era of TikTok-oriented quickies that just kind of peter out after a couple of promising mini-choruses. Lyrically, the verses explore deranged jealousy, but unlike Taylor Swift's 'Blank Space,' Rodrigo's narrator isn't campy but vulnerable. She explains that she thought her current lover would be 'the antidote this time,' but he's not 'the cure' because he has nothing to do with what's wrong with her.

A few days ago, Rodrigo shared on Instagram that this new single is 'my favorite song on the album and one of my favorite songs I've ever made.' She also described it as the album's thesis statement. 'the cure' is the second single from her June 12th album you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love, following 'drop dead.' That first single debuted at number one, so expectations for the album are sky-high. Rodrigo premiered the video and single at the Metrograph Theatre in New York City, followed by a fan Q&A. The music video, directed by Cat Solen and Jamie Gerin, features Rodrigo as an old-school nurse running experiments, with body-horror imagery including organs turning from red to grey and the singer bleeding red yarn. June 12 cannot come soon enough.
keyPoints: Olivia Rodrigo released 'the cure,' the second single from her album you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love, out June 12
Despite the name, Rodrigo confirmed the song is not about or inspired by the band The Cure
Critics say the track sounds more like The Smashing Pumpkins, particularly Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness
Rodrigo called 'the cure' her favourite song on the album and its thesis statement
The first single 'drop dead' debuted at number one, raising big expectations for the album
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Olivia Rodrigo you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love album

articles.title: Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters Sorry for Calling Staff 'Lower-Value Human Capital'
articles.summary: Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters was forced to apologise after publicly saying the bank would replace 'lower-value human capital' with AI, sparking a global backlash. The comments came as Standard Chartered announced plans to cut around 7,800 back-office jobs by 2030. Winters said sorry for the upset but stopped short of taking back what he actually said.
articles.content:
Some words really cannot be unsaid. Bill Winters, the CEO of the British multinational bank Standard Chartered, said something so viscerally off-putting that he went into full damage control mode. Speaking at an investor event in Hong Kong, Winters tried to explain that cutting thousands of jobs was not really about saving money. Here is how he put it: "It's not cost-cutting," he said. "It's replacing, in some cases, lower-value human capital with the financial capital and the investment capital we're putting in." The phrase quickly sparked backlash from employees and observers, many of whom viewed the wording as dismissive and dehumanising.

The controversy follows the bank's announcement of plans to cut approximately 7,800 back-office positions, one of the earliest large-scale workforce reductions by a major global bank explicitly linked to the growing use of AI technologies. To put that in context, among the bank's 81,000 employees and 17,000 contract workers, roles in human resources, risk, and compliance are expected to see the deepest cuts.

The internet was not kind. The Financial Times's Alphaville blog mocked Winters' remarks by selling "lower-value human capital" merchandise to "celebrate your looming obsolescence." Even the former president of Singapore, Halimah Yacob, weighed in, writing that it was "disturbing to read workers described as 'lower-value human capital.'" Regulators also came knocking. Hong Kong and Singapore regulators sought clarification from the bank about Winters' remarks.

Winters issued an apology, but it was a careful one. Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters apologised for the upset caused to staff by his remarks, but stopped short of retracting the comments. In his latest post, Winters included a transcript of his full remarks, which he said showed that he valued his colleagues "most highly" and included preceding context that the bank was "giving every opportunity" to at-risk employees who want to learn new skills. Not everyone was convinced. One commenter wrote: "This was either a poor choice of words or an honest belief that came out as intended."

Winters is far from the only banking boss eyeing major workforce reductions. Rival lender HSBC revealed in March that it was weighing sweeping layoffs that could impact as many as 20,000 employees. The planned cuts are reportedly tied to a broader push to replace some human roles with artificial intelligence. The Winters story is really just the moment someone accidentally said out loud what a lot of executives are quietly thinking.
keyPoints: Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters called workers set to lose their jobs 'lower-value human capital'
Standard Chartered plans to cut around 7,800 back-office jobs by 2030 as part of an AI push
Winters apologised for the upset but did not retract the substance of his comments
Hong Kong and Singapore regulators asked the bank for clarification
HSBC is separately considering cutting up to 20,000 jobs in a similar AI-driven restructuring

articles.title: Ed Sheeran Leaves Warner Music After 15 Years on Good Terms
articles.summary: Ed Sheeran has parted ways with Warner Music Group after 15 years and eight studio albums, announcing the split directly to fans via his newsletter. Sheeran was clear this was not a falling-out, calling it a personal decision driven by how much his life has changed since he was a teenager. Rumours are already circling that Universal Music Group could be his next home.
articles.content:
Ed Sheeran has left Warner Music after 15 years and eight full-length albums, announcing the news via a statement to fans in his newsletter. Warner Music is the only major label Sheeran has ever worked with, so this is a genuinely big deal in the music world.

Sheeran was quick to shut down any drama. "This isn't a 'disgruntled artist leaves record label' type situation," he wrote. With an estimated 200 million albums sold since his 2011 debut "+" and one of the most-streamed songs in history with "Shape of You," which has accumulated nearly 5 billion streams on Spotify alone, Sheeran is one of the world's top recording artists. He explained that he is simply a different person now to the teenager who first signed. "My life is hugely different now to what it was when I was a teenager, and I've been feeling in my gut for a long time that a lot of things in my professional life need to change," he said.

The story of how Sheeran ended up at Warner in the first place is a good one. He landed with Warner after a chance meeting with Ed Howard, formerly head of A&R for Asylum, at a show in Notting Hill. Sheeran was 18 and ended up crashing at Howard's house, playing him music and chatting about life. He honestly did not know Howard worked at a record label at the time, thinking he was just someone cool letting him sleep on his sofa. The labels went on to oversee the release of seven albums, including Multiply, Divide, Subtract, Equals, and the new series starter, Play.

While the rights to his newer releases are held by his own Gingerbread Man label and licensed to Warner, his Warner catalog will remain with the company in a "continued partnership," and the licensed releases are part of what has been described as a long-term distribution deal. So the split is clean but not completely cold.

Part of what drove the change is that many of the executives Sheeran worked closely with have already left Warner, including former Warner recorded-music chief Max Lousada and former Atlantic Music Group CEO Julie Greenwald, who recently announced the launch of their own 26.2 record label through Sony Music. Rumours are already swirling that Sheeran has struck a lucrative deal with Universal Music Group, though a rep for Universal did not immediately confirm this. Watch this space.

Sheeran's Play album kicks off a series that will continue with Rewind, Fast-Forward, and Stop, so wherever he lands next, there is plenty of new music coming. For a guy who describes himself as "underneath it all, a singer-songwriter who plays pub gigs," the next chapter looks very interesting.
keyPoints: Ed Sheeran has left Warner Music Group after 15 years, ending the only major label relationship of his career
Sheeran says the split is entirely amicable and driven by personal growth, not conflict
He has sold an estimated 200 million albums and Shape of You alone has nearly 5 billion Spotify streams
His back catalogue stays with Warner, and his recent Gingerbread Man label releases remain under a long-term distribution deal
Universal Music Group is rumoured to be his next label home, though nothing has been confirmed

articles.title: SpaceX Launches Starship V3 But Loses Super Heavy Booster
articles.summary: SpaceX successfully launched Starship V3 for the first time on May 22, 2026, in what was the program's 12th test flight overall. The Ship upper stage completed most of its planned objectives and splashed down in the Indian Ocean as planned, but the Super Heavy booster failed its boost-back burn and crashed into the Gulf of Mexico. The milestone matters enormously because Starship V3 is the rocket SpaceX needs for everything from Starlink satellite launches to a NASA crewed Moon landing in 2028.
articles.content:
SpaceX pulled off a mostly successful debut of Starship V3 on Friday, May 22, launching the upgraded megarocket from its new Pad 2 at Starbase in south Texas. Liftoff of Integrated Flight Test-12 was on-time at 5:30 pm Central Time from Starbase, Texas. After a scrub the day before caused by a hydraulic pin on the launch tower arm that failed to retract, SpaceX got everything sorted overnight and made the launch window with no drama.

The Ship upper stage lost one engine but performed most of its planned objectives and landed in the Indian Ocean as planned. The Super Heavy booster failed to perform its boost-back burn correctly and crashed into the Gulf of Mexico, though it was not planned to be recovered on this flight. So the ship half of the vehicle did its job. The booster did not. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk still called it a win, congratulating the SpaceX team on an "epic first Starship V3 launch and landing" and saying the team "scored a goal for humanity."

It was the 12th launch in the Starship series, but the first of Version 3, with completely redesigned Raptor engines and other significant upgrades. Starship V3 can carry more than 100 metric tons to orbit in reusable configuration, which is roughly three times what the previous version could handle. That jump in capability is not just impressive on paper. It is the whole reason this rocket exists. NASA needs Starship to work in order to serve as the lander for its Artemis 4 astronauts during a lunar landing mission in 2028, and SpaceX is relying on the fully reusable Starship to dramatically lower launch costs for its human spaceflight projects, Starlink internet satellites, and plans for orbital data centers.

SpaceX previously said that if all goes well with Flight 12, Flight 13 may be an orbital launch, but that has yet to be determined. The path to Starship V3 followed five flights of Version 2 in 2025 and extensive testing that included two separate explosive setbacks on the test stand, which destroyed a Super Heavy booster and a Starship. NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman was at Starbase to watch the launch in person. The booster loss is a setback, but the overall picture is positive. SpaceX now has a brand new launch pad and a rocket that just proved it can fly.
keyPoints: Starship V3 flew for the first time on May 22 in Flight 12, the program's 12th test overall
The Ship upper stage splashed down in the Indian Ocean as planned despite losing one engine
The Super Heavy booster crashed into the Gulf of Mexico after its boost-back burn failed
Starship V3 can carry over 100 metric tons to orbit, roughly triple the previous version's capacity
NASA needs Starship V3 to work as the Human Landing System for a crewed Moon mission in 2028

articles.title: CBS News Radio Signs Off After 99 Years With Murrow's Voice
articles.summary: CBS News Radio broadcast its final newscast on Friday, May 22, 2026, closing nearly a century of American broadcast journalism. The sign-off ended with the recorded voice of legendary journalist Edward R. Murrow delivering his famous farewell, 'Good night, and good luck.' The network, which started in 1927 and once reached 32 million listeners weekly, cited challenging economic realities for the shutdown.
articles.content:
CBS News Radio went dark on Friday night after 99 years on the air, and it did not go quietly. The final newscast closed with the voice of Edward R. Murrow, the journalist who essentially built CBS News Radio's identity, signing off with his legendary phrase 'Good night, and good luck.' It was the perfect ending to a story that stretched back nearly a century.

Anchor Christopher Cruise delivered the final broadcast at 11:31 PM ET, with a special newscast focused on the network's legacy and history. Earlier that morning, anchor Steve Kathan had hosted the final edition of the World News Roundup, the longest-running newscast in the United States. During that last roundup, Kathan played a clip of Edward R. Murrow making his on-air debut from Vienna on March 13, 1938, reporting on the Nazi invasion of Austria. That original broadcast is what started the World News Roundup format in the first place.

CBS News Radio traces its roots to 1927, when the Columbia Broadcasting System launched as a network. At its peak it reached 32 million listeners every week and was carried on around 700 affiliate stations across the country. It won seven consecutive Radio Television Digital News Association awards for overall excellence from 2007 to 2013, a record that has never been matched. Current and former staffers gathered at the network's broadcast center on Friday evening to mark the end together, with between 60 and 70 jobs being eliminated by the closure.

So why did it end? CBS News Radio's parent company, Paramount Skydance, announced in March that it was shutting the network down, citing what it called 'challenging economic realities' and a shift in radio programming strategies. Fewer people are tuning into traditional radio, and the new owners are focused on streaming. The Writers Guild of America East called the decision 'reckless and shortsighted.'

Former CBS Evening News anchor Dan Rather, who got his start in radio, reflected on what the network meant to him and to the country. Harvey Nagler, who ran CBS News Radio from 1998 to 2016, described the closure as 'another substantive blow to our democracy.' Some former CBS News Radio talent are moving to Red Apple Media's new Worldwide News Network, which launched at midnight, so some of that institutional knowledge will carry on. But the original, the one that Edward R. Murrow built, is gone.
keyPoints: CBS News Radio signed off permanently on May 22, 2026 after 99 years on air
The final broadcast ended with the recorded voice of Edward R. Murrow saying 'Good night, and good luck'
Anchor Christopher Cruise hosted the final newscast at 11:31 PM ET
The network once reached 32 million listeners weekly across around 700 affiliate stations
Parent company Paramount Skydance cited challenging economic realities for the closure
Between 60 and 70 staff positions were eliminated

articles.title: Oura Files for IPO at an $11 Billion Valuation
articles.summary: Oura, the smart ring maker, has confidentially filed a draft Form S-1 with the SEC, putting itself on a path to go public. The company hit an $11 billion valuation after a $875 million Series E round last year, and CEO Tom Hale has said Oura could generate close to $2 billion in sales in 2026. Half of the 5.5 million Oura rings ever sold shipped in just the last twelve months, which tells you how fast this thing is taking off.
articles.content:
Oura, the Finnish-American smart ring company, just made its biggest move yet. On Thursday May 21, Oura confidentially submitted a draft registration statement on Form S-1 with the Securities and Exchange Commission, officially starting the clock on a potential IPO. The company did not set a date, saying it will move forward after the SEC finishes its review and market conditions look right.

The numbers behind this filing are hard to ignore. Oura was valued at $11 billion last October after raising $875 million in a Series E funding round, more than double the $5 billion valuation it carried in 2024. CEO Tom Hale told CNBC in November that Oura could generate close to $2 billion in sales in 2026. The company reported revenue above $500 million in 2024, then was on track to hit $1 billion in 2025, essentially doubling year over year.

Oura is not alone in this space, but it has carved out a clear lead. Whoop, a direct rival, raised $575 million at a $10.1 billion valuation in March. Apple keeps adding health features to Apple Watch, and Google recently announced a screenless Fitbit. But Oura's ring form factor and explosive growth have kept it at the front of the pack. The company now partners with more than 1,200 health, wellness, and commercial brands, and it recently moved its headquarters from Finland to San Francisco.

For the IPO itself, Oura has lined up Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan, Allen & Co., and Jefferies to run the deal, per Bloomberg. The number of shares and the price range have not yet been set. One of the bigger questions for public market investors will be whether Oura gets priced like a consumer hardware company or like a health-tech platform. That distinction could mean a very different valuation once the full S-1 becomes public.
keyPoints: Oura confidentially filed a Form S-1 with the SEC on May 21, 2026, signaling a coming IPO.
The company was valued at $11 billion after a $875 million Series E round last year.
CEO Tom Hale projects Oura could hit nearly $2 billion in revenue in 2026.
Half of all 5.5 million Oura rings ever sold shipped in just the past twelve months.
Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and JPMorgan are among the banks lined up to run the deal.

articles.title: Niall Horan's 'End of an Era' Is His Goodbye to Liam Payne
articles.summary: Niall Horan released 'End of an Era' on Friday May 22, an emotional tribute to his late One Direction bandmate Liam Payne, who died in October 2024 at age 31. The song is the third single from Horan's forthcoming fourth solo album, Dinner Party, out June 5. What makes the story even more moving is that Horan and his co-writers did not initially realise the song was about Payne until producer Julian Bunetta pointed it out mid-session.
articles.content:
Niall Horan has found a way to say the goodbye he never got to say. Horan released his new song 'End of an Era,' which is about the grief he experienced after the death of his friend and former One Direction bandmate Liam Payne. Payne died in October 2024 at the age of 31 following a fall from his hotel balcony in Buenos Aires, Argentina. The loss shook fans of the group worldwide and left the remaining One Direction members publicly grieving in different ways.

What makes 'End of an Era' such a compelling story is how it came to be. When Horan was working on songs for his upcoming fourth solo album, Dinner Party, he said he was 'dancing around' the idea of writing a song in honour of Payne, trying to figure out how to pay tribute to him. He told the And the Writer Is podcast that he wrote the track with John Ryan and Julian Bunetta at Bunetta's studio in Nashville. The trio kept working on the song but something was not clicking. Then came the breakthrough moment. Horan explained how co-writer Julian Bunetta helped him realise that the song they were writing was about Liam. Bunetta said: 'This song is about Liam. We just don't know it yet. We're dancing around it subconsciously.' And then the song was written in five minutes.

Horan initially wrote the record with the intention of 'looking back on the past with nostalgia, being happy with what you had, excited for what's going to happen,' but returned to it with a new perspective following Payne's death. The result is a song that carries both emotions at once. One verse is sad and one verse is nostalgic, and they are two feelings Horan has when he thinks of Liam. Horan co-wrote 'End of an Era' with Julian Bunetta and John Ryan, who both first met him and Liam during their One Direction days. The two songwriters also worked on One Direction's fifth studio album, Made in the A.M., and had lived next door to Liam for a period of time.

'End of an Era' will appear on Horan's upcoming album, Dinner Party, out June 5, but Horan wanted to share the song in advance of the album to give it some breathing room. He wrote on social media: 'I hope it means as much to you as it does to me. I felt it needed its own space before the album comes out in a few weeks, so here you go.' It is a rare kind of pop song. Celebratory and heartbroken at the same time, it manages to honour a friendship without turning grief into spectacle. That is a hard thing to pull off, and Horan pulls it off.
keyPoints: Niall Horan released 'End of an Era' on May 22 as a tribute to Liam Payne, who died in October 2024
The song was co-written with Julian Bunetta and John Ryan in Nashville
Horan and his co-writers did not initially realise the song was about Payne until Bunetta pointed it out mid-session
'End of an Era' is the third single from Horan's fourth album, Dinner Party, out June 5
Horan released it ahead of the album so the song could have its own space

articles.title: Google's AI Thinks "Disregard" Is a Command, Not a Word
articles.summary: Google Search's big AI overhaul has produced an embarrassing bug: typing the word "disregard" causes the Gemini-powered AI to respond as if you issued it an instruction, leaving a giant blank space where a definition should be. The glitch exposes a core flaw in Google's most ambitious Search redesign in 25 years. It also gives Bing — yes, Bing — a rare moment to look better than Google.
articles.content:
Google just made one of the most dramatic changes to its Search product in over two decades. At Google I/O 2026 this week, the company officially killed the era of the ten blue links and replaced them with an AI-first experience powered by its Gemini model. Conversational answers come first now. Traditional results get pushed way down the page. It is, by any measure, a massive bet.

But the rollout hit a weird wall almost immediately. Typing "disregard" into Google Search causes Google's AI to interpret it as a system-style instruction instead of a search query. The result? "Understood. Message disregarded." Instead of a definition, you get a chatbot response that belongs in a developer console, not a dictionary lookup. The Merriam-Webster link is still technically in there, but you have to scroll past a huge block of empty space, and for most users, that single AI reply is the only thing they'll see.

Google's AI Overviews appear to be breaking on several known AI command words, including "disregard", "dismiss", and "ignore." Words like "remember", "start", "finished", and "forget" are also causing the AI problems. The reason is straightforward: these are words commonly used to instruct AI chatbots, and Google's Gemini system is confusing a normal search query for a prompt. The AI Overview replaces the expected definition with a prompt-style reply, and adding the word "definition" to your search does not always fix it.

The same search on Bing works considerably better. TechCrunch writer Russell Brandom noted that was a striking reversal. A Google spokesperson confirmed that Google is aware AI Overviews are misinterpreting some action-related queries, and that the company is working on a fix. The bug will likely get patched fast. But it has already become a viral symbol of what happens when you rush one of the world's most-used products into an AI-first transformation at scale. Just a month ago, a study found that AI Overviews are wrong 1 in 10 times, and the problem could be even worse. Google has a lot of trust left to rebuild.
keyPoints: Google's new Gemini-powered Search treats 'disregard' as an AI instruction and replies 'Understood. Message disregarded.' instead of showing a definition
The same bug affects other command-style words like 'dismiss', 'ignore', 'forget', and 'cancel'
Google confirmed the issue and said a fix is in progress
Bing handles the same search correctly, giving it a rare win over Google
The bug is a symptom of Google's biggest Search redesign in 25 years, announced at Google I/O 2026

articles.title: Red Dead Redemption 2 Is Now the 3rd Best-Selling Game Ever
articles.summary: Take-Two Interactive CEO Strauss Zelnick revealed during the company's May 2026 earnings call that Red Dead Redemption 2 has now sold over 85 million copies worldwide, making it the third best-selling video game of all time. Even more jaw-dropping: Rockstar's 2018 open-world western just had its highest annual sales since its launch year, beating out Wii Sports and The Sims without a single major content update. This is all happening while the entire gaming world is staring at Grand Theft Auto 6.
articles.content:
Nobody is talking about Red Dead Redemption 2 right now. Everyone is waiting for Grand Theft Auto 6. And yet, quietly, without a big marketing push or a major update, Rockstar Games' sweeping 2018 western just hit one of the biggest milestones in the history of video games.

"Rockstar Games' Red Dead Redemption 2 achieved its highest level of annual unit sales since its launch year, with over 85 million units sold to date," said Take-Two CEO Strauss Zelnick during the company's Q4 fiscal 2026 earnings call on May 21. Read that again. A game released in 2018 just had its best sales year outside of its own launch year. That is not a normal thing that happens.

Red Dead Redemption 2 is now the third best-selling game of all time in the entire medium. The open-world western has outdone games that are widely regarded as mainstream phenomena, like Wii Sports and The Sims. It sits right behind GTA 5 in second place with 230 million units sold and Minecraft in first place with a staggering 350 million units sold. Between March and May alone, Red Dead Redemption 2 sold over 3 million copies, which is on average how much it typically sells each quarter. The game has been running at that pace for years. Quietly. Without anyone noticing.

What makes this even stranger is the context. Unlike GTA Online, which has had update after update since its launch, Red Dead Online stopped receiving regular updates in 2022. Zelnick made a curious distinction on the call, characterizing GTA Online as a "live service business" while labelling Red Dead Online and by extension Red Dead Redemption 2 as a "legacy live service." A legacy live service. Selling 3 million copies a quarter. Without any new content.

Zelnick said that GTA Online and Red Dead Redemption 2 alike are games that "have proven to be vastly more resilient than anyone expected, and I think it's a reflection of the quality of the work that Rockstar has done." In total the Red Dead series has now sold nearly 115 million copies, up from 110 million just three months ago. Meanwhile Take-Two is projecting around $8 billion in revenue for fiscal 2027, driven largely by the launch of Grand Theft Auto 6. Red Dead Redemption 2 is the quiet giant that nobody is watching. And it keeps winning.
keyPoints: Red Dead Redemption 2 has now sold over 85 million copies, making it the 3rd best-selling game of all time
Take-Two CEO Strauss Zelnick revealed the game just had its highest annual sales since its 2018 launch year
It outsells Wii Sports and The Sims despite no major content updates since 2022
Between March and May 2026 alone, Red Dead Redemption 2 sold over 3 million copies
The entire Red Dead series has now sold nearly 115 million copies worldwide

articles.title: Rob Base, Creator of 'It Takes Two,' Dead at 59
articles.summary: Rob Base, the rapper behind the 1988 hip-hop classic 'It Takes Two,' died on Friday, 22 May 2026, following a private battle with cancer. His passing was confirmed by his official Instagram account. Base was one of hip-hop's most recognisable voices, and 'It Takes Two' became one of the genre's most sampled and enduring party anthems.
articles.content:
Rob Base died on Friday at the age of 59. His official Instagram account confirmed the news, saying he had lost a private battle with cancer. No further details about his illness or a memorial service were shared at the time of posting.

Base was born Robert Ginyard in Harlem, New York, and came up through the borough's vibrant hip-hop scene in the 1980s. He teamed up with DJ E-Z Rock and together they released 'It Takes Two' in 1988. The song sampled Lyn Collins' 'Think (About It)' and became a massive crossover hit, landing at number three on the Billboard Hot 100 and selling over two million copies in the US alone. It remains one of the best-selling singles in hip-hop history.

'It Takes Two' never really went away. Decades after its release, the track kept showing up at sporting events, weddings, film soundtracks, and commercials. It became the kind of song that needs no introduction at any party. That kind of staying power is genuinely rare, and it speaks to just how perfectly constructed that record was.

Rob Base continued to perform and connect with fans throughout his career, even as the spotlight shifted to newer acts. He was a Harlem original who helped define what hip-hop could sound like when it was built purely for joy. The culture lost a real one today.
keyPoints: Rob Base died on Friday 22 May 2026 after a private battle with cancer
His death was confirmed via his official Instagram account
He was 59 years old
Rob Base was best known for the 1988 hit 'It Takes Two,' one of hip-hop's most enduring anthems
'It Takes Two' sold over two million copies in the US and has been a pop culture staple for nearly four decades

articles.title: Stephen Colbert's Late Show Finale Draws 6.74M Viewers, a Series Record
articles.summary: Stephen Colbert closed out The Late Show With Stephen Colbert on Thursday night with the most-watched weeknight episode in the show's history, drawing 6.74 million live and same-day viewers. CBS confirmed the number beats every previous weeknight episode, including earlier high-water marks from Colbert's tenure. It is a remarkable send-off for a show that CBS cancelled in July 2025, citing what it called a purely financial decision.
articles.content:
Stephen Colbert said goodbye to The Late Show With Stephen Colbert on Thursday night, and America showed up in a massive way. The finale pulled in 6.74 million live and same-day viewers, according to preliminary panel-only data from Nielsen. CBS confirmed that makes it the most-watched weeknight episode in the entire run of The Late Show With Stephen Colbert. That is a record the show had never hit before, even during its biggest cultural moments.

To put that number in context, late-night television has been losing viewers for years as audiences shift to streaming and short-form video. Pulling nearly 7 million people to a live network broadcast in 2026 is a genuine feat. It says a lot about how much affection audiences had built up for Colbert and the show over more than a decade.

The Late Show With Stephen Colbert launched in 2015 when Colbert took over from David Letterman. CBS cancelled the show in July 2025 in what the network called a purely financial decision, though the timing was hard to separate from the political climate around Colbert's frequent and pointed commentary on Donald Trump. The cancellation set off a wave of fan support and media conversation that kept the show in the spotlight right up to its final weeks.

The send-off was fittingly celebratory. In the days leading up to the finale, The Late Show With Stephen Colbert hosted a string of notable guests, including David Byrne, who performed the Talking Heads classic "Burning Down the House" with Colbert himself joining in on stage in a matching blue suit. CBS cancelled The Late Show in July 2025, citing it as "purely a financial decision." The record-breaking finale audience suggests that whatever the reason for the cancellation, the show went out as beloved as ever.
keyPoints: The Late Show With Stephen Colbert finale drew 6.74 million live and same-day viewers
CBS confirmed it is the most-watched weeknight episode in the show's entire history
CBS cancelled the show in July 2025, calling it a purely financial decision
The finale capped a run of high-profile farewell guests and performances
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Late Show with Stephen Colbert TV series

articles.title: Madonna Scores First No. 1 Radio Hit in 18 Years With 'I Feel So Free'
articles.summary: Madonna's 'I Feel So Free' has reached the top of Billboard's Dance/Mix Show Airplay chart, her first No. 1 radio hit since 'Miles Away' in 2008. The track is the opening salvo from her upcoming album Confessions II, due July 3 via Warner Records. It climbed to the top spot in just five weeks after debuting at No. 14 on the chart.
articles.content:
Madonna is back on top. 'I Feel So Free' has just hit No. 1 on Billboard's Dance/Mix Show Airplay chart, making it Madonna's first radio chart-topper in 18 years. The last time she sat at the summit of a Billboard airplay chart was with 'Miles Away' back in 2008. That is a long time coming, and the dance floors are very happy about it.

'I Feel So Free' is the first taste of Confessions II, Madonna's forthcoming fifteenth studio album arriving July 3 via Warner Records. She previewed it in a 60-second clip on April 15, then it premiered exclusively on iHeartRadio's Pride Radio on April 17 before hitting all streaming platforms the following day. It debuted at No. 14 on the Dance/Mix Show Airplay chart, which Billboard noted was the fifth highest debut on that chart in the entire 2020s. Five weeks later, it is sitting at number one.

The song itself is an EDM deep house track that critics have compared to Donna Summer classics like 'Love to Love You Baby' and 'I Feel Love', as well as Madonna's own 2005 track 'Future Lovers' from Confessions on a Dance Floor. That connection is no accident. Confessions II reunites Madonna with Confessions on a Dance Floor producer Stuart Price, with Venezuelan musician Arca also on board as co-producer. The Guardian called it 'subtly appealing, exceptionally well made,' adding that it sounds like Madonna being herself rather than chasing trends.

Confessions on a Dance Floor, released in 2005, was one of the defining dance albums of the 2000s and spawned Madonna's first four No. 1s on the Dance/Mix Show Airplay chart. Confessions II already has 16 tracks loaded on Spotify and a South Korean DJ Peggy Gou remix of 'I Feel So Free' dropped on May 15. If this first single is any indication, the Queen of Pop is not easing herself back in. She is kicking the door down.
keyPoints: Madonna's 'I Feel So Free' is now No. 1 on Billboard's Dance/Mix Show Airplay chart
It is her first radio No. 1 in 18 years, since 'Miles Away' in 2008
The song is the opening track from her upcoming album Confessions II, out July 3 via Warner Records
Confessions II reunites Madonna with Confessions on a Dance Floor producer Stuart Price
A Peggy Gou remix of 'I Feel So Free' was released on May 15, 2026

articles.title: Tom Hardy Fired From MobLand After Clashes Pushed Creator to Quit
articles.summary: Tom Hardy will not return for MobLand Season 3 after clashing with executive producer Jez Butterworth and 101 Studios during Season 2 production. Things got so heated that Butterworth threatened to quit, and Paramount chose to drop Hardy rather than lose the show's creator. It is not the first time Hardy has been at the centre of on-set drama, most famously with Charlize Theron during the making of Mad Max: Fury Road in 2015.
articles.content:
Tom Hardy is out of MobLand. Paramount has decided not to pick up the 48-year-old British actor's option for Season 3, following a string of behind-the-scenes clashes during the making of Season 2. Hardy clashed with producers Jez Butterworth and David Glasser, among others, during the making of Season 2. The situation escalated fast.

Things got so bad that Butterworth threatened to quit. Paramount responded by dropping Hardy instead of losing Butterworth. That is a very loud statement about who the studio values more. Hardy was reportedly regularly late to set and "constantly asked to give notes on scripts." On top of that, Hardy was said to be displeased over MobLand becoming more of an ensemble series built around Helen Mirren, Pierce Brosnan, and the rest of the cast, instead of remaining centered around him.

For anyone new to MobLand, it is a London crime drama on Paramount+. Hardy plays fixer Harry Da Souza in the series, which launched on Paramount+ last spring and quickly became the second most-watched series on the platform. MobLand was created by Ronan Bennett and stars Pierce Brosnan, Helen Mirren, and Paddy Considine. It follows the Harrigan crime family, headed by Brosnan's Conrad and his wife, Maeve, played by Mirren. Hardy was also an executive producer on the show, making this exit even messier.

There is one interesting wrinkle: Hardy's contract included a mutual option for Season 3, meaning Hardy also could have walked away on his own if he wanted. So it is hard to say exactly who left whom. This is not the first time Hardy has clashed with a co-star on set. He and Charlize Theron infamously feuded on the set of Mad Max: Fury Road. The two actors starred in the 2015 post-apocalyptic drama together and were known to have not gotten on, with Theron admitting that the pair went at it during filming. A third season has not been formally announced, but given the show's success it is all but guaranteed. How Hardy's character would be written out of the series remains to be seen.
keyPoints: Tom Hardy will not return as Harry Da Souza in MobLand Season 3
Hardy clashed with executive producer Jez Butterworth and producer David Glasser during Season 2
Butterworth threatened to quit, and Paramount chose to keep Butterworth over Hardy
Hardy was reportedly unhappy that MobLand was shifting toward an ensemble focus around Mirren and Brosnan
Hardy has a prior history of on-set conflict, most notably with Charlize Theron on Mad Max: Fury Road

articles.title: Mums Using Cranial Manipulation and Beef Ribs to 'Looksmaxx' Their Babies
articles.summary: A growing number of parents, led by figures like Leisa Mari Bekkers, are applying extreme physical and dietary techniques to their children in pursuit of conventional beauty ideals. Practices range from cranial manipulation and so-called thumb-pulling to reshape facial features, to beef rib-heavy diets intended to build a strong jawline. The trend borrows heavily from the adult looksmaxxing movement, which originated in online communities focused on maximising physical attractiveness.
articles.content:
Looksmaxxing is the practice of systematically optimising your appearance, taking everything from jaw exercises to skincare routines to extreme levels. It blew up in online communities over the past few years, and now some parents are applying the same logic to their babies and toddlers before those children are old enough to have any say in the matter.

Leisa Mari Bekkers is one of the most prominent voices in this space. She is raising two teenagers, aged 14 and 19, whom she describes as her life's work, and neither has ever taken a pharmaceutical drug. For Bekkers and parents like her, shaping a child's physical development is treated as a full-time, long-term project that begins well before the child can walk or talk.

The techniques involved are striking. Cranial manipulation involves applying physical pressure to a baby's or young child's skull, based on the belief that the bones are still soft enough to be shaped. Thumb-pulling is a practice aimed at reshaping facial structure, with parents manually manipulating a child's face in hopes of achieving a more symmetrical or defined look. A beef rib-heavy diet is promoted for building a strong jawline, with the tough, chewy texture of the meat supposedly giving young jaw muscles a workout that leads to better definition over time.

The looksmaxxing movement that inspired this trend started in online forums obsessed with the idea of rating physical attractiveness and achieving an idealised look. It has now spawned a huge amount of content on TikTok and YouTube, with tutorials, progress videos, and community challenges. The leap from adults doing jaw exercises to parents managing their children's diets and skull shape is a dramatic one, and critics have raised serious questions about whether any of these techniques are safe or effective, let alone appropriate to impose on a child.

What makes this trend culturally significant is what it reveals about how deep beauty anxiety now runs. When the desire to be attractive stops being a personal choice and starts being a project parents carry out on their children from birth, it tells you something about how the pressure to look a certain way has intensified. Whether this is fringe behaviour or a sign of something broader growing in parenting communities is a question worth watching.
keyPoints: Parents are applying adult looksmaxxing techniques to babies and young children in pursuit of conventional beauty ideals
Leisa Mari Bekkers is raising two teenagers, aged 14 and 19, using these methods as a core part of her parenting philosophy
Techniques include cranial manipulation, thumb-pulling to reshape facial features, and a beef rib diet for jawline development
The looksmaxxing movement originated in online forums focused on maximising physical attractiveness and has exploded on TikTok and YouTube
Critics are raising concerns about the safety, effectiveness, and ethics of applying these practices to children

articles.title: Rafael Nadal Gets Standing Ovation at Rafa Netflix Premiere in Madrid
articles.summary: Rafael Nadal received a standing ovation at the world premiere of his Netflix docuseries Rafa in Madrid on Wednesday, joined by his family and a star-studded crowd. The four-part series, directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Zach Heinzerling, hits Netflix globally on May 29. Nadal admitted he had turned down documentary offers for years before finally saying yes to this one.
articles.content:
Rafael Nadal is one of the greatest athletes who ever lived, and now he finally has a documentary to match that legacy. The 22-time Grand Slam winner appeared at the world premiere of Rafa in Madrid on Wednesday night, where he was joined by his dad Sebastián Nadal, his mom Ana Maria Parera, his uncle Toni Nadal, who coached him for years, and Carlos Moyá, who coached Nadal in the final years of his career. Guests also included television journalist Christiane Amanpour and Late Night host Seth Meyers. The evening concluded with an exclusive screening of the first episode, which received a long standing ovation from attendees in a premiere filled with emotion and celebration.

The Rafa premiere was held at the historic Beti Jai fronton sports venue in the Spanish capital. The venue, opened in 1894, was transformed into a massive outdoor theater for the event, with an extra-large screen and seating set up for guests to enjoy a preview of the documentary, which is one of the most highly anticipated Netflix releases of the season.

The four-part series, which premieres on Netflix on May 29, is directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Zach Heinzerling, known for Cutie and the Boxer and Stolen Youth: Inside the Cult at Sarah Lawrence. The release is perfectly timed during the French Open, the tournament where Nadal won a record 14 titles. From his historic dominance on the Paris clay courts to the rivalry that defined a generation of tennis alongside Roger Federer and Novak Djokovic, Rafa revisits the defining moments of his career while revealing the man behind the legend: the pain, the constant pressure, and the determination to keep moving forward even when his body seemed unable to continue.

Before the Madrid premiere, Nadal spoke with Deadline about the project, admitting he had resisted previous documentary overtures many times. "I refused to do it plenty of times in my tennis career," he said, explaining he agreed this time because he found the right people and because filming began in 2024, the last stage of his career. He described Rafa as "an intimate look into what my career has been like, with details that were previously unknown and a touch of reality regarding what the life of a professional tennis player is like with various injury struggles."

Injuries caused Nadal to miss competition at 18 Grand Slams, preventing him from adding to his remarkable total of 22. He retired in 2024 at the age of 38 and turns 40 on June 3. Director Zach Heinzerling said, "With Rafa's story, triumph is expected; what surprised me was his willingness to reveal the uncertainty and vulnerability behind that greatness." After decades of watching Nadal fight through pain on court, we are finally about to see what it all really looked like from the inside.
keyPoints: Rafael Nadal attended the world premiere of his Netflix docuseries Rafa in Madrid on Wednesday, receiving a standing ovation
The four-part series drops globally on Netflix on May 29, timed during the French Open
Rafa is directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Zach Heinzerling and produced by Skydance Sports
Nadal said he had turned down documentary offers many times before agreeing to this one
The series covers his career from age three all the way through his retirement in November 2024
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Rafael Nadal tennis career Grand Slam
